body_txt: Fictional ghosts are often depicted wearing heavy chains, shackles, and other bindings. This typically serves as a way to represent the mental and metaphorical fetters and obsessions that continue to haunt them after death and keep them bound to the mortal world. In some cases, as a ghost resolves or moves on from their Unfinished Business , their chains may visibly break or slough off when the specter is freed and able to move on. A form popularized in A Christmas Carol has these chains instead representing a ghost's evil deeds, cursing the damned to stagger under the literal weight of their evil forever. Visually, the chains may be further weighed down with objects symbolizing the ghost's specific obsessions and faults; the most famous version is the ghost of the greedy, miserly Marley dragging moneyboxes and safes at the end of his chains, but theoretically, anything could be used in this fashion. Specters bound in this manner are often depicted as noisily dragging and rattling their chains as they wander their haunts, adding this noise to whatever other groaning, moaning, or weeping they may make. Not to be confused with Jacob Marley Apparel , which refers to the dead continuing to wear ghostly copies of the clothing that they had on at death or during their funerals and maintain whatever injuries killed them. Compare Haunted Fetter , which describes objects that sympathetically bind a ghost to the world of the living, and Mummy Wrap . This is almost always present in Yet Another Christmas Carol stories. Subtrope of Chained by Fashion and Our Ghosts Are Different . The variant where a ghost is chained to reminders of their faults and sins overlaps with Ironic Hell .
